exercises.title: Identify expensive method calls in loops
exercises.instructions:
For each, identify if the method call inside the loop makes it O(n) or O(n²):
A: `for x in list: hashSet.contains(x)`
B: `for x in list: arrayList.contains(x)`
C: `for x in list: treeMap.get(x)`
D: `for x in list: arr[i]`
exercises.expected_solution:
A: O(n) total — hashSet.contains(): O(1) avg → n × O(1) = O(n)
B: O(n²) total — arrayList.contains(): O(n) scan → n × O(n) = O(n²)
C: O(n log n) total — treeMap.get(): O(log n) → n × O(log n) = O(n log n)
D: O(n) total — arr[i]: O(1) index → n × O(1) = O(n)

Key lesson: the DATA STRUCTURE inside the loop determines the outer complexity.
Always check: what is the complexity of that method call?

test_cases.expected:
A: O(n) total — hashSet.contains(): O(1) avg → n × O(1) = O(n)
B: O(n²) total — arrayList.contains(): O(n) scan → n × O(n) = O(n²)
C: O(n log n) total — treeMap.get(): O(log n) → n × O(log n) = O(n log n)
D: O(n) total — arr[i]: O(1) index → n × O(1) = O(n)

Key lesson: the DATA STRUCTURE inside the loop determines the outer complexity.
Always check: what is the complexity of that method call?

hints: HashSet.contains(): O(1). ArrayList.contains(): O(n)
TreeMap.get(): O(log n) — Red-Black Tree
Array index: always O(1)

exercises.title: Write recurrence relations for recursive functions
exercises.instructions:
Write the recurrence relation for each:
A: Binary search (halves array each call)
B: Merge sort (splits in 2, merges in O(n))
C: Linear search (checks one element, recurses on rest)
D: Fibonacci naive (two calls, each on n-1 and n-2)
exercises.expected_solution:
A: Binary search: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) → solves to O(log n)
   Each call: 1 comparison + recurse on half the array

B: Merge sort: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) → solves to O(n log n)
   Two recursive calls on halves + O(n) merge step

C: Linear search: T(n) = T(n-1) + O(1) → solves to O(n)
   Each call: 1 comparison + recurse on n-1 elements
   Expands: O(1)×n calls = O(n)

D: Fibonacci naive: T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + O(1) → O(2ⁿ)
   Exponential: each call branches into 2, depth n → 2ⁿ nodes
   This is WHY naive Fibonacci is catastrophically slow

test_cases.expected:
A: Binary search: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) → solves to O(log n)
   Each call: 1 comparison + recurse on half the array

B: Merge sort: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) → solves to O(n log n)
   Two recursive calls on halves + O(n) merge step

C: Linear search: T(n) = T(n-1) + O(1) → solves to O(n)
   Each call: 1 comparison + recurse on n-1 elements
   Expands: O(1)×n calls = O(n)

D: Fibonacci naive: T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + O(1) → O(2ⁿ)
   Exponential: each call branches into 2, depth n → 2ⁿ nodes
   This is WHY naive Fibonacci is catastrophically slow

hints: T(n) = T(n/2) + constant: halving → O(log n)
T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n: two halves + linear work → O(n log n)
T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2): exponential branching → O(2ⁿ)

exercises.title: Annotate code with Big-O comments
exercises.instructions: Add Big-O comments to each line of this Razorpay payment processor. Then state the overall complexity.
hints: Arrays.sort(): O(n log n)
HashMap.put/getOrDefault: O(1) average
Two-pointer while loop: O(n) — lo and hi move toward each other

exercises.title: Analyze recursive Fibonacci — find the exponential trap
exercises.instructions: Implement naïve recursive Fibonacci. Add a call counter to show how many function calls are made for fib(10), fib(20), fib(30). Compare to 2ⁿ and prove why memoization is critical.
hints: Call counter: static int incremented in every fib() call
fib(30) makes ~2M calls — visible exponential growth
Memoization: store computed values, avoid recomputation

exercises.title: Complexity of sorting + processing pipeline
exercises.instructions:
Zerodha pipeline: (1) sort trades by amount, (2) remove duplicates, (3) find the kth largest.

Analyze and implement:
1. Sort: O(n log n)
2. Deduplicate using HashSet: O(n)
3. Find kth largest from sorted array: O(1) — by index

Print each step's complexity and verify the pipeline produces correct output.
hints: Sort first → deduplicate is easy (consecutive duplicates)
kth largest in sorted array: index from end, O(1)
Pipeline complexity: dominated by the most expensive step

exercises.title: Analyze three approaches to 'Find Pair with Sum'
exercises.instructions:
Three approaches to find if any pair sums to target:
1. Brute force: O(n²)
2. Sort + two pointers: O(n log n)
3. HashSet: O(n)

Implement all three. Verify same results. Print complexity for each.
hints: Two pointers: requires sorted array first
HashSet: check complement exists before adding
Trade-off: O(n) time uses O(n) space; O(n log n) two-pointer uses O(1) extra space

exercises.title: Analyze method chains and hidden costs
hints: subList(): O(1) — backed view, no copy
computeIfAbsent + HashSet.add: both O(1) average
Nested loops over n: O(n²) — optimize with HashMap frequency counting

exercises.title: Recursion tree analysis — power function
exercises.instructions:
Analyze and implement three versions of computing a^n:
1. Naive: a*a*a... n times → O(n)
2. Recursive halving (fast power): a^n = a^(n/2) * a^(n/2) → O(log n)
3. Java's Math.pow() — explain complexity

Draw the recursion tree for fast power with n=8, print depth, verify correctness.
hints: Key insight: fastPower(base, n/2) computed ONCE, then squared — not called twice
exp % 2 == 0: square the half; exp % 2 != 0: multiply by base
Recursion depth = log₂(n) — each call halves the exponent

exercises.title: Detect O(n²) in production code and fix it
hints: Original code: nested loop O(n²) + result.contains O(n) = O(n³)!
Fix: one-pass HashMap building stock sets per trader
HashSet.contains(): O(1) — use for membership checks in loops

exercises.title: Analyze processPayments() from the hook
exercises.expected_solution:
[A] Collections.sort(): O(n log n) — TimSort on n payments
    Dominant step for the sort phase

[B] HashMap declaration: O(1) — no work, just allocation

[C] for (Payment p : payments): O(n) iterations — loop over all n payments

[D] computeIfAbsent + list.add(): O(1) average — HashMap op + ArrayList append
    Loop [C]+[D] total: O(n) × O(1) = O(n)

[E] for (var entry : byMerchant.entrySet()): O(k) iterations where k = unique merchants
    k ≤ n (at most n unique merchants for n payments)

[F] reconcile(list): O(m log m) where m = payments for that merchant
    Sum across ALL merchants: Σ(mᵢ log mᵢ) ≤ Σ(mᵢ × log n) = n log n
    (because mᵢ ≤ n so log mᵢ ≤ log n)
    Loop [E]+[F] total: O(n log n)

TOTAL:
  [A] O(n log n) sort
  [C-D] O(n) map building
  [E-F] O(n log n) reconcile
  Combined: O(n log n) + O(n) + O(n log n) = O(n log n)

FINAL ANSWER: O(n log n)
Interview answer: 'This is O(n log n) dominated by the initial sort and
the per-merchant reconciliation, which collectively sort n payments across all merchants.'

test_cases.expected:
[A] Collections.sort(): O(n log n) — TimSort on n payments
    Dominant step for the sort phase

[B] HashMap declaration: O(1) — no work, just allocation

[C] for (Payment p : payments): O(n) iterations — loop over all n payments

[D] computeIfAbsent + list.add(): O(1) average — HashMap op + ArrayList append
    Loop [C]+[D] total: O(n) × O(1) = O(n)

[E] for (var entry : byMerchant.entrySet()): O(k) iterations where k = unique merchants
    k ≤ n (at most n unique merchants for n payments)

[F] reconcile(list): O(m log m) where m = payments for that merchant
    Sum across ALL merchants: Σ(mᵢ log mᵢ) ≤ Σ(mᵢ × log n) = n log n
    (because mᵢ ≤ n so log mᵢ ≤ log n)
    Loop [E]+[F] total: O(n log n)

TOTAL:
  [A] O(n log n) sort
  [C-D] O(n) map building
  [E-F] O(n log n) reconcile
  Combined: O(n log n) + O(n) + O(n log n) = O(n log n)

FINAL ANSWER: O(n log n)
Interview answer: 'This is O(n log n) dominated by the initial sort and
the per-merchant reconciliation, which collectively sort n payments across all merchants.'

hints: Label each line with its cost
Sum across all merchants: Σmᵢ = n
Amortized: reconcile across all merchants is O(n log n) total, not O(n × n log n)

exercises.title: Implement merge sort and verify O(n log n)
exercises.instructions:
Implement merge sort from scratch. Count comparison operations. Verify that for n=1024, comparisons ≈ n × log₂(n) ≈ 10240.

This is the canonical O(n log n) sorting algorithm asked in FAANG interviews.
hints: l + (r-l)/2: avoids integer overflow vs (l+r)/2
Arrays.copyOfRange: creates subarray for merge step
ratio ≈ 0.5-1.0: proves actual comparisons match theoretical O(n log n)

exercises.title: Complexity of string operations in loops
hints: String immutability: += creates new object each time
StringBuilder: mutable buffer, append is amortized O(1)
n=10000: O(n²) should be noticeably slower

exercises.title: Analyze and solve: Group Anagrams (Flipkart interview)
exercises.instructions:
Given n words, group all anagrams together.
Example: ['eat','tea','tan','ate','nat','bat'] → [['eat','tea','ate'],['tan','nat'],['bat']]

1. Naive: O(n² × m) where m = average word length
2. Optimized: O(n × m log m) — sort each word as key
3. Optimal: O(n × m) — use character frequency as key

Implement all three. Verify correct. Print complexity for each.
hints: Sorted key: 'eat','tea','ate' all sort to 'aet' — same key
Frequency array: [1,0,0,...,1,0,0,...] — 26 letters, count occurrences
Arrays.toString(freq): converts int[] to string key for HashMap

exercises.title: Complexity of recursive descent — tree traversal
exercises.instructions:
Implement and analyze 3 ways to sum all values in a binary tree:
1. Recursive DFS: what is the complexity?
2. Iterative BFS with Queue: what is the complexity?
3. Which is faster and why?

Build a tree of n nodes, verify both give same sum, print complexity.
hints: Both DFS and BFS visit every node exactly once → O(n)
Recursion depth for DFS: O(h) stack frames
BFS queue max size: O(w) where w = max width of tree

exercises.title: Complete M29 T2 Code Analysis Synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete analysis toolkit:
1. Implement and compare O(n²) vs O(n log n) vs O(n) for a single problem:
   'Find all pairs of trades where trade[i] + trade[j] = target'
2. Return ALL pairs (not just first one)
3. Count comparisons in each approach
4. Print analysis table and verify all solutions agree
5. @SpringBootTest: 4 tests pass

expected_stdout_contains: All find same unique pairs: true

hints: Normalize pairs: Pair(min,max) so (3,7) and (7,3) are treated as same
Set<Pair>: eliminates duplicates automatically
All 3 should find same unique sum pairs
